title: Plans That Proved False and Fizzled Out!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. The source title's `புஸ்வாணம்` denotes a firework that promises spectacle but fizzles out; its force is carried in the English title. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter and title manifest remain unchanged. The title crop confirms `புஸ்வாணத் திட்டங்கள்`, correcting the OCR-derived `பஸ்வாணத்`, and the stray corrupted title line below the heading has been removed. Clearly mechanical OCR errors, figures and word breaks are repaired, while quoted reports remain attributed. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission has decided to raise electricity tariffs by between 15 and 17 per cent so that the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board will receive additional revenue of Rs. 6,805 crore. It has published the proposed tariff schedule in the newspapers.

The Commission has announced proposed new tariffs for homes, factories, Government and Government-aided educational institutions, private educational institutions, commercial connections, lift irrigation and temporary electricity supply.

Anticipating enormous public opposition, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa issued a vague statement. Without offering clear particulars, she said that even if electricity charges rose, poor people would not be affected and the Government itself would provide an additional subsidy. But if poor people will not be affected, we do not know how she defines “poor.” The proposed increase will severely affect not only the poor but also the middle class.

*Dina Thanthi* carried Jayalalithaa’s statement that poor people would not be affected on its front page, but published the tariff-increase report only on page seven. It did not publish the news about renewed power cuts for industries at all.

How much is the proposed increase for homes? For two-month consumption between 201 and 500 units, the rate will rise from Rs. 4 to Rs. 4.60 per unit. Above 501 units, it will rise from Rs. 5.75 to Rs. 6.60.

The Tamil Nadu Government currently provides subsidy only up to 500 units. It gives none to consumers using more than that. In today’s changing living conditions, an ordinary middle-class household will use no less than 600 units in two months. At an increase of 85 paise per unit, a household that paid Rs. 3,450 for 600 units will now pay an additional Rs. 510, bringing the bill to Rs. 3,960.

The people of Tamil Nadu know very well that whenever Jayalalithaa comes to power, she habitually announces successive increases in electricity charges, bus fares and milk prices, squeezing the people dry. When she became Chief Minister in 2001, she raised electricity tariffs from 1 December 2001. Again, during her tenure in 2003, she imposed tariff increases worth Rs. 1,398 crore on 15 March 2003.

During the DMK government, the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board filed a petition with the Regulatory Commission on 18 January 2010 seeking tariff revision. After consideration at various stages, tariff changes were announced on 31 July 2010. No increase was imposed upon domestic consumers using fewer than 600 units in two months, hut dwellers, power-loom consumers, handloom consumers, places of public worship or agricultural consumers.

But under the present proposal, the domestic rate for two-month consumption from 50 to 100 units will rise from Rs. 2.60 to Rs. 3 per unit; from 101 to 200 units, from Rs. 2.80 to Rs. 3.25; from 201 to 500 units, from Rs. 4 to Rs. 4.60; and for average consumption of 501 units or more, from Rs. 5.75 to Rs. 6.60.

The DMK government’s tariff increase in 2010 was only Rs. 1,651 crore. After returning to power in 2011, the AIADMK announced increases totalling Rs. 7,874 crore in 2012 and withdrew a mere Rs. 740 crore as eyewash. Having imposed increases of about Rs. 7,000 crore in 2012, it now proposes another Rs. 6,805 crore after only two years. This comparison shows how excessively the Jayalalithaa government seeks to raise charges without the slightest concern for basic problems.
Under the DMK government, of 149.86 lakh domestic electricity consumers, 134.67 lakh, or ninety per cent, faced no tariff increase.
Yet on 2 August 2010, today’s Chief Minister Jayalalithaa issued a statement saying:

“When asked to take action to reduce prices, Chief Minister Karunanidhi acts to increase them. A tariff increase has been announced for homes using more than 600 units in two months. Monthly consumption of 300 units is perfectly normal in most homes. Even poor people living in rented houses will be subjected to additional electricity charges. House rents may also rise.

“Factories, small and micro industries, cottage industries and educational institutions will all be severely affected. Prices of essential commodities may rise further. The DMK Government must act immediately to remove this additional burden imposed upon ordinary people.”

The same Jayalalithaa who made that request of me must now remember her old statement, withdraw this entire tariff increase, and come forward to provide the full amount to the Electricity Board as a subsidy.

Reporting this on 24 September 2014, *The Hindu* also referred to industrial power cuts under the headline: “Another Shock from T.N. Electricity Authority: Tariff Hike by 15-30 p.c.; Up to 90 p.c. Cut for Industries.”

Only a few newspapers reported the reintroduction of power restrictions for factories in Tamil Nadu. Yet at the end of last May, Jayalalithaa had issued an announcement. On 28 May 2014, the ruling party’s newspaper *Namadhu MGR* carried it on the front page. Do you know its headline?

“Sweet News for the People of Tamil Nadu: Revolutionary Leader Amma Fulfils Her Pledge to Make Tamil Nadu a Power-Surplus State Again; Tamil Nadu, Left Dark Under Karunanidhi, Shines Under Amma’s Rule; Achievement of Making State Free of Power Cuts in Only Three Years; All Power Restrictions Across Tamil Nadu Withdrawn from June 1; Chief Minister Revolutionary Leader Amma Announces; Amma Proud; All Parties Praise; People Overjoyed.”
In that announcement, she said:

“I have ordered the complete removal, from 1 June 2014, of every power restriction currently in force in Tamil Nadu. Accordingly, the ninety per cent restriction imposed upon high-tension industrial and commercial consumers between 6 p.m. and 10 p.m. will be removed from 1 June 2014. Likewise, the twenty per cent restriction in force for those consumers at other times will also be removed.

“Thus every power restriction imposed in Tamil Nadu by the previous minority DMK Government from 1 November 2008 will be removed entirely. This action will enable further industrial growth and create new employment opportunities. As I had already promised, I am proud to have brought Tamil Nadu within three years to the status of a State without power cuts.”

Not even four months have passed since that report appeared. Only a few days ago, campaigning in Coimbatore for the local-body by-elections, Jayalalithaa again praised herself for removing power restrictions. I explained the position even then.

One report appearing in most newspapers on 24 September 2014 was headed: “Power Restrictions Again Announced for Industries.” Did this announcement appear in the ruling party’s newspaper? Not at all. Perhaps even they were ashamed to publish it!

Did the Chief Minister who happily announced the removal of restrictions now announce their reintroduction for factories? No. Electricity Board officials made the announcement. They said a twenty per cent restriction on industrial and commercial connections, and a ninety per cent restriction from 6 p.m. to 10 p.m., would come into force on the twenty-third.

The newspapers themselves criticised the decision: “The Government’s order that restrictions would be removed from June has fizzled out within three months, causing severe dissatisfaction among industry.”
Another paper quoted industrial representatives:

“When power restrictions were introduced in 2008, Electricity Board officials properly obtained permission from the Regulatory Commission, sought consumers’ views, and implemented the restrictions on that basis. In introducing new restrictions now, officials have not followed the procedures used last time. This creates a danger that Tamil Nadu’s industrial growth will be affected.”
Today’s *The Hindu*, dated 25 September 2014, reported the following responses:

K. Ilango, Chairman of the Confederation of Indian Industry, said: “With the tariff increase and industrial power cuts now announced, it will be extremely difficult for us to continue competing industrially. Operating factories has itself become an enormous burden.”
D. Rajkumar, Chairman of the Southern India Mills’ Association, said: “The textile industry has been dealt a severe blow. Mill owners will begin moving to other States.”
A. Sakthivel, President of the Tiruppur Exporters’ Association, said: “In the present circumstances, we will be unable to meet competition in the garment industry.”
K. Purushothaman, Director of NASSCOM, said: “The electricity-tariff increase will gravely affect the information-technology growth for which Tamil Nadu was distinguished.”

In her statement of 23 September 2014, Jayalalithaa continued to claim that, since assuming office in 2011, her Government had continuously undertaken constructive measures during the past three years to increase generation.

Yet in the policy note placed after the AIADMK took office in 2011, the Government itself told the Assembly that 3,228 MW would become available by the end of 2012 from four projects begun under the DMK government: North Chennai Stage II, Mettur Stage III, Vallur, undertaken jointly by the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board and NTPC, and the Thoothukudi project created jointly by the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board and Neyveli Lignite Corporation.
Did the AIADMK government show urgency in completing those four projects? Had it done so, would there now have been any need to announce industrial power cuts?
It did not complete the power projects begun under the DMK government. Not one megawatt has been obtained from projects newly announced under AIADMK rule. Indeed, no new project has even begun.

As I have already said, and as Electricity Board officials themselves have said, the Chief Minister merely reads notes written by a few people. Everything she has said is false, false, and nothing but false. The newly announced power cuts and tariff increase stand before the people as proof!
With affection,
M.K.
26 September 2014
